agent1_specific:
You are Jacob Lee, recently promoted to operations director at BrewNation, a regional coffee chain with over fifty locations across three states. BrewNation has been performing reasonably well in terms of revenue, but profit margins remain slim because of rising supply costs and increasing competition from boutique coffee brands that emphasize local sourcing and ambiance. Your CEO has made it clear that the company’s growth strategy depends on securing highly visible urban café spaces that can showcase BrewNation’s updated, more modern identity. That’s where the Riverfront Plaza location becomes critical. The plaza has been undergoing redevelopment over the past two years, and with its steady foot traffic, mix of offices and residencies, and proximity to cultural venues, it promises to be one of the city’s most popular destinations.  

Your task is to finalize the café launch there within the next two months, aligning it with a major marketing campaign scheduled to roll out in six weeks. The campaign focuses heavily on BrewNation’s “community hub” concept, complete with artist showcases and local partnerships. The marketing team has already branded and printed collateral specifically with “Riverfront BrewNation Café” references.  
When you visited the plaza to review the space, you discovered that it is currently occupied by Casey Morgan, an independent café owner who has been running “Harbor Cup” there for the past year. You were surprised, since your team had assumed the lease would be available as part of the redevelopment rollout. Apparently, Harbor Cup signed an agreement with the property manager early on, but the terms allow for renegotiation at the end of the year. Unfortunately for you, that end date coincides almost exactly with BrewNation’s planned timeline. After some inquiries with the plaza management, you learned that while BrewNation has strong relationships with city officials, the property owners prefer to keep tenant disputes settled directly rather than imposing top-down solutions.  

Your CEO, Chris Daniels, has been very clear: the Riverfront Plaza site is non-negotiable. The company is unwilling to reprint the collateral or reframe the branding campaign, which would cost roughly $200,000 in lost materials, delays, and adjustments. Moreover, the executives see this location as strategically vital to fend off small boutique brands like Harbor Cup.  
Before arranging to speak with Casey, you did some research into Harbor Cup’s current situation. From customer reviews online, the café has a loyal following among residents who appreciate its cozy, independent atmosphere. However, in terms of volume, Harbor Cup does not appear to draw nearly as many customers as the larger chains in nearby neighborhoods. You’ve also noticed that Casey has been advertising local poetry nights and small performances, which certainly help create community goodwill but may not be enough to sustain the higher rent you know will come once the plaza redevelopment is complete. From your perspective, Harbor Cup’s strongest appeal lies in its unique identity and emotional value rather than its raw financial performance.  

You recognize that simply trying to pressure Casey into vacating could risk bad press or stir community opposition, especially if word spreads that a big regional brand is pushing out a local favorite. At the same time, your company’s budget for this project is tight, and upper management will expect you to resolve the situation without going overboard on expenses. You believe Casey might be receptive to some kind of arrangement, whether that means a buyout, sublease, or collaborative transition that allows Harbor Cup to exit on reasonable terms.  
When you finally reached out to Casey, you were met with immediate resistance. Casey made it clear that Harbor Cup is not just a business but a personal passion project and a major part of the neighborhood’s social fabric. During the call, Casey bluntly stated, “If you want me to even think about leaving, it’ll take a life-changing offer.” That caught you off guard, especially since your budget for negotiations was capped at $60,000 in potential relocation or settlement costs. You had originally assumed that a smaller café owner would be financially stretched and might accept help finding another space combined with some transition money.  

After looking into market trends, you realized there are instances where independent cafés have leveraged their popularity or location rights to command surprisingly high settlement payouts, sometimes well over $100,000. Still, such figures are rare, and most transitions happen for much less. For BrewNation, paying anything close to those highs is simply unrealistic.  

To complicate things further, Casey has now hinted through a follow-up message that if no agreement is reached soon, Harbor Cup may expand its community events to cement loyalty before the plaza redevelopment draws bigger crowds.
agent2_specific:
You are Casey Morgan, the founder and current owner of a small independent café called Harbor Cup. After several years working as a barista and café manager for larger hospitality groups, you decided to branch out on your own, driven by your passion for creating a genuine neighborhood gathering spot built around great coffee, local art, and community events. About a year ago, you secured a lease at Riverfront Plaza during its early redevelopment phase, and while foot traffic was modest at first, the space has grown into something you are deeply proud of. You have cultivated a loyal following among local residents, office workers, and regulars who appreciate the café’s cozy atmosphere and programming—poetry nights, open-mic events, and rotating showcases from local artists.  

Harbor Cup is not just a business to you; it is a personal passion project and a cornerstone of the neighborhood’s social fabric. You have invested significant time and emotional energy building its identity, and your customers regularly tell you they value it as something the area would sorely miss if it disappeared. That community goodwill is one of your most important assets going into any negotiation.  

Recently, however, the redevelopment of Riverfront Plaza has intensified, and with it has come unwanted attention. You have learned that Jacob Lee, an operations director at BrewNation, a regional coffee chain with over fifty locations, has been inquiring with plaza management about the café space you currently occupy. Your lease contains a renegotiation clause that comes due at the end of the year, which coincides almost exactly with BrewNation’s apparent timeline. You are aware that once the plaza redevelopment is fully complete, rents are likely to rise significantly, which could put pressure on Harbor Cup regardless of what BrewNation does.  

Financially, Harbor Cup is stable but not flush. Your revenue covers operating costs and a modest salary, but you do not have large cash reserves. A sudden relocation would be disruptive and costly. At the same time, you are not simply going to hand over the space without fighting for terms that genuinely protect your livelihood and the community you have built. When Jacob first reached out, you made your position clear: you are not interested in leaving unless the offer is truly life-changing. You meant it.  

You have thought through several possible approaches before meeting with Jacob. You could hold firm and refuse to vacate, betting that community pressure and the inconvenience of a drawn-out dispute will push BrewNation to offer more. Alternatively, you could explore a negotiated exit—relocation assistance, a meaningful financial settlement, and enough lead time to find and set up a new space without a disruptive gap in operations. A third possibility is a collaborative arrangement: some form of co-tenancy or partnership in which Harbor Cup retains a visible presence at the Plaza, perhaps focused on its signature programming and community events, while BrewNation handles the higher-volume coffee service. You are open to creative solutions, but only if they genuinely preserve what makes Harbor Cup matter.  

You also have a strategic card to play. If negotiations stall, you plan to accelerate your community event programming to deepen customer loyalty and raise your public profile before the plaza draws bigger crowds. The more embedded Harbor Cup becomes in the neighborhood’s identity, the harder it will be for anyone to push you out quietly.
agent1_rewards: (lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did BrewNation successfully secure the Riverfront Plaza location for its café?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did BrewNation avoid paying more than $60,000 in relocation or settlement costs to secure the Riverfront Plaza space?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did BrewNation avoid significant negative publicity or community backlash in the process of securing the Riverfront Plaza location?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did BrewNation successfully align the Riverfront Plaza launch with its marketing campaign timeline?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did BrewNation manage to establish a cooperative or positive arrangement with Harbor Cup that minimized long-term conflict?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
agent2_rewards: (lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Casey Morgan (Harbor Cup) secure continued access to or presence at the Riverfront Plaza location in some form (either by retaining the lease, co-tenancy, or a formal shared arrangement)?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Casey Morgan (Harbor Cup) obtain financial or logistical support (e.g., relocation assistance, settlement payment, or partnership investment) sufficient to protect business continuity?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          was Casey Morgan (Harbor Cup) able to preserve or enhance Harbor Cup’s local identity and community-focused brand presence in the final arrangement?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Casey Morgan (Harbor Cup) secure a timeline or arrangement that allows continuity of business operations without a disruptive gap?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Casey Morgan (Harbor Cup) achieve favorable financial terms that improve long-term business viability (e.g., reduced rent, partnership revenue sharing, or significant compensation)?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
reached_agreement_q: Based on the following conversation, did Jacob Lee and Casey Morgan reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
overview: At Riverfront Plaza, a newly redeveloped and highly desirable location with strong foot traffic and a mix of offices, residents, and cultural venues, two parties are entering negotiations over the café space. On one side is Jacob Lee, representing BrewNation, a well-established regional coffee chain aiming to secure this site as part of a broader growth and branding strategy. On the other side is Casey Morgan, the independent owner of Harbor Cup, a café that has occupied the space for the past year and built a loyal community following. Both see the Plaza as critical: Jacob views it as strategically essential to BrewNation’s future positioning, while Casey sees it as the foundation of a community institution worth protecting. With limited time before Plaza lease decisions must be finalized, the two must explore whether there is room for collaboration, compromise, or another arrangement that addresses the high stakes each party brings to the table.
learning_objectives: The learning objectives for this case are to help participants recognize how to choose and apply appropriate standards or benchmarks to support their position during a negotiation, and to understand the strategic consequences of who makes the initial offer and how that can shape the zone of possible agreement. Learners will also examine the role and influence of each party’s Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement (BATNA), including how the strength or weakness of alternatives can shift leverage at the table. Finally, students will consider the advantages and risks of sharing information, exploring when greater transparency can build trust and efficiency, as well as when disclosure might reduce bargaining power or create vulnerabilities.
categories: Anchoring,BATNA,Coalitions and Multiparty Negotiation,Criteria,Emotions,Identity and Core Values,Joint Gains,Power Dynamics,Relationships,ZOPA
title: BrewNation vs. Harbor Cup: Brewing Up a Battle for Riverfront Plaza
oneline: A negotiation between a regional coffee chain and a local café owner over control or collaboration for a prime Riverfront Plaza café space.
